title: Graphs
description: Graphs in Go use map[int][]int adjacency lists. Master DFS, BFS, and connected components — these patterns solve the majority of graph interview problems.

sections.title: Building an adjacency list
sections.explanation: Represent graphs as **map[int][]int** — each key maps to its list of neighbors. Build from an edge list by appending to both directions (undirected) or one direction (directed). This is the standard representation for interview graph problems.

exercise.prompt: Build an undirected adjacency list from edges [[0,1],[0,2],[1,2],[2,3]]. Print the neighbors of node 0 and node 2.

validation.message: Node 0 connects to [1 2], node 2 connects to [0 1 3]
Use map[int][]int and append in both directions for undirected

exercise.hint: For undirected: append e[1] to graph[e[0]] and e[0] to graph[e[1]].

sections.title: DFS on graphs
sections.explanation: Graph DFS uses a **visited set** to avoid cycles. Start from a node, mark visited, recurse on unvisited neighbors. Use a closure or pass the visited map as a parameter. DFS finds connected components, detects cycles, and performs topological sort.

exercise.prompt: Perform DFS from node 0 on graph: 0->[1,2], 1->[3], 2->[3], 3->[4], 4->[]. Print each node as it's visited.

validation.message: DFS explores depth-first: 0->1->3->4, then backtracks to 2
Mark visited, check before recursing, call dfs on unvisited neighbors

exercise.hint: Set visited[node] = true first. Check !visited[nb] before dfs(nb).

sections.title: BFS on graphs
sections.explanation: Graph BFS uses a **queue** for level-order exploration. It finds the shortest path in unweighted graphs. Enqueue the start, mark visited, then loop: dequeue, process, enqueue unvisited neighbors. Track distance with a level counter or distance map.

exercise.prompt: Find shortest distances from node 0 in graph: 0->[1,2], 1->[3], 2->[4], 3->[5], 4->[5], 5->[]. Print the distance to node 5.

validation.message: Shortest path from 0 to 5 is 3 (0->1->3->5 or 0->2->4->5)
Set distance as parent+1, check !seen, enqueue neighbor

exercise.hint: Check !seen for unvisited. dist[nb] = dist[node] + 1 computes the shortest distance.

sections.title: Connected components
sections.explanation: Count connected components by iterating all nodes and running DFS/BFS from each unvisited one. Each new DFS/BFS call discovers a new component. This pattern is used in island counting, friend circles, and union-find alternatives.

exercise.prompt: Count connected components in an undirected graph with 7 nodes (0-6). Edges: [0,1],[1,2],[3,4],[5,6]. Print the number of components.

validation.message: Run DFS from each unvisited node and increment the component counter

exercise.hint: Call dfs(nb) for unvisited neighbors. Check !visited[i] in the outer loop. Increment components++ each time.
